source.definition: to identify and fix mistakes or inaccuracies

explanation.sourceText: Derived from the Latin verb 'corrigere', meaning 'to make right' or 'to improve', composed of 'cor-' (together) and 'regere' (to guide or rule), implying the act of setting something straight or making it accurate.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „correct“ provine din verbul latin „corrigere“, care înseamnă „a face ceva corect“ sau „a îmbunătăți“, format din „cor-“ (împreună) și „regere“ (a conduce sau a ghida), sugerând actul de a corecta sau a face ceva precis.

source.additional: Regular verb. Commonly used in educational and professional contexts to indicate the action of fixing errors or making improvements.

target.definition: a rectifica sau a îmbunătăți o eroare

explanation.sourceText: Stammt vom lateinischen „corrigere“, was „richtig machen, verbessern“ bedeutet und eng mit dem Wort „corect“ (korrekt, richtig) verbunden ist.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a corecta“ derivă din latinul „corrigere“, care înseamnă a face ceva corect sau a îmbunătăți, legat de adjectivul „corect“.

target.additional: Regelmäßiges Verb der 1. Konjugation (-a). Wird verwendet, um Fehler zu beheben oder um etwas in Ordnung zu bringen.

explanation.targetText: Folosit în context educațional pentru corectarea greșelilor.
Pentru a corecta sau a remedia neînțelegeri.

examples.sourceText: Are you correcting the exams today?
They correct their papers on time.
examples.targetText: Ei corectează lucrările lor la timp.

examples.sourceText: He corrected the records every day.
examples.targetText: El corecta înregistrările în fiecare zi.

examples.sourceText: I will correct the mistakes in the work.
I would correct it if I had the time.
examples.targetText: Aș corecta, dacă aș avea timp.

learningTips.text: The English word 'correct' and Romanian 'corecta' share Latin roots, making them easy to connect.
Think of 'correct' as setting something straight, just like fixing a mistake on a test or paper.
Remember that 'corecta' is often used in school or work contexts when you want to improve or fix errors.
The sound 'cor-' in both words can remind you of 'core' or 'heart'—getting to the heart of the issue by fixing it.
